steps.title: Founding of Rome
steps.description: Seven hills, a river crossing, a handful of Latin villages. Nothing here announces what is coming. But the city that grows from this spot will absorb the Mediterranean world, codify its laws, carry its languages into the future — and its collapse will shape Europe more than its existence ever did.

steps.title: Republic to Empire
steps.description: For two centuries the republic held together — barely. Then a generation of civil wars tore it apart. Caesar crossed the Rubicon, was killed, and his adopted heir finished what he started. In 27 BC, the Senate hands Octavian a cluster of special powers. He takes the name Augustus. The republic doesn't end with a bang — it quietly becomes something else.

steps.title: Rome at Its Height
steps.description: Under Trajan, the empire reaches its greatest extent — from Scotland to Mesopotamia, from the Rhine to the Sahara. Forty million people live under Roman law. The roads, aqueducts, and legal codes are already ancient. But size is also a vulnerability: the longer the border, the more it costs to defend, and the more there is to lose.

steps.title: Fall of the Western Empire
steps.description: Romulus Augustulus is deposed and the last Roman emperor of the West vanishes into obscurity. At the time, few notice — the empire has been dissolving for decades. What matters is what fills the void: Germanic kingdoms, the Catholic Church, and a broken road network that once connected everything. Europe will spend the next four centuries trying to reassemble the pieces.

steps.title: Charlemagne Crowned
steps.description: On Christmas Day 800, Pope Leo III crowns Charlemagne Emperor in St. Peter's Basilica. The act fuses three things that will define European politics for a millennium: Germanic military power, the legacy of Rome, and the authority of the Church. For the first time since 476, a single ruler controls most of Western Europe — but it won't last long.

steps.title: Norman Conquest of England
steps.description: William defeats Harold at Hastings. The conquest grafts Norman French culture, continental feudalism, and Roman legal thinking onto Anglo-Saxon England — producing a hybrid that will generate common law, parliamentary government, and a language spoken by a billion people. The Europe that emerges from this moment is recognizably the one that will go on to shape the modern world.

steps.title: Peace of Westphalia
steps.description: Thirty years of religious war across Central Europe — perhaps a third of the German population dead. The treaties signed in Osnabrück and Münster in 1648 don't just end a war. They establish a new principle: that states, not churches, are the sovereign actors in international affairs. The modern international order begins here, in the smoking rubble of the Holy Roman Empire.

steps.title: Founding of Rome
steps.description: We begin in Rome — but we are watching the East. The city on the Tiber will grow into an empire that spans the known world. That empire will generate an idea: one law, one civilization, one Rome. What this trail follows is what happens to that idea when Rome itself is gone.

steps.title: Constantine Moves East
steps.description: Constantine founds a new capital on the Bosphorus and names it after himself. He also legalizes Christianity. In one reign, the empire shifts its center of gravity eastward and changes its religion. The Latin West and the Greek East begin to drift — not yet apart, but in different directions. Rome doesn't fall. It moves.

steps.title: Fall of the West
steps.description: Romulus Augustulus is deposed in Ravenna. In Constantinople, the emperor continues to rule. The Eastern court does not call this the fall of Rome — they are Rome. They will call themselves Romans for another thousand years. What collapses in the West is seen from the East as a regional setback, not the end of anything.

steps.title: Justinian's Reconquest
steps.description: Justinian reconquers North Africa, Italy, and parts of Spain. He codifies Roman law. He builds the Hagia Sophia. He speaks Latin and calls himself Roman. But his empire is Greek-speaking, Christian, and Eastern. He is the last emperor who still thinks like a Roman — and after his death, most of what he recaptured is lost again. The old Rome cannot be rebuilt. Something new is taking its place.

steps.title: Arab Expansion
steps.description: In 637, Jerusalem falls. Within a generation, Syria, Egypt, and North Africa follow — the wealthiest provinces of the empire, gone. What remains is Anatolia, Greece, and the Balkans. The empire survives, but it is no longer Mediterranean. Stripped of its richest lands, it is forced inward. It is becoming something else.

steps.title: The Great Schism
steps.description: The Pope in Rome and the Patriarch in Constantinople excommunicate each other. The split has been building for centuries — different languages, different liturgy, different politics. After 1054, the Eastern Church is Orthodox, not Catholic. This is the moment Byzantium stops being a variant of Rome and becomes fully itself: a Greek-speaking, Orthodox Christian civilization with its own identity, its own theology, and its own future.

steps.title: Fall of Constantinople
steps.description: Mehmed II breaches the walls. Constantine XI dies in the fighting — the last Roman emperor, defending a city founded by the first Christian emperor, eleven centuries later. The empire is gone. But the Orthodox Church survives. Russian princes claim the Byzantine legacy. Greek scholars carry their manuscripts to Italy, where they help ignite the Renaissance. An empire ends. An idea doesn't.

steps.title: The First Words
steps.description: In southern Mesopotamia, temple administrators need to track grain and livestock. They press a reed stylus into clay and make marks. At first it is accounting — not literature, not prayer, just numbers. But the marks become symbols, the symbols become words, and the words become something no one anticipated: a way to send thoughts across time. Writing is not invented. It grows from a practical problem.

steps.title: Signs of the Nile
steps.description: Almost simultaneously, Egypt develops its own system — hieroglyphs. Whether it emerged independently or was sparked by contact with Mesopotamia is still debated. What is clear is that the two systems are fundamentally different in form. Cuneiform is abstract wedges on clay. Hieroglyphs are pictorial, carved in stone, painted on walls. Same idea, different world. Writing is already proving it can take many shapes.

steps.title: The Alphabet is Born
steps.description: Phoenician merchants on the eastern Mediterranean coast develop something radical: an alphabet of just 22 signs. No pictures, no ideograms — only sounds. No vowels either, just consonants, leaving the reader to fill in the rest. But the reduction from hundreds of signs to twenty-two changes everything. Writing is no longer the domain of scribes who spend years in training. It becomes learnable. The alphabet is not beautiful. It is efficient — and that is why it conquers the world.

steps.title: Greece Adds a Voice
steps.description: The Greeks borrow the Phoenician alphabet — and immediately change it. Phoenician has no vowels; Greek does, and the language cannot be written without them. So the Greeks repurpose letters they don't need as consonants and use them for vowels instead. A small technical adjustment with enormous consequences: for the first time, a writing system can capture spoken language with full precision. Homer is written down. Philosophy becomes possible. The Greek alphabet is not just adapted — it is completed.

steps.title: Rome Writes the World
steps.description: Rome does not take the alphabet from Greece directly. It comes via the Etruscans — an older Italian civilization that had already adapted Greek script for their own language. Rome inherits it, simplifies it, and then spreads it across an empire stretching from Britain to Mesopotamia. Latin becomes the language of law, administration, and the Church. When the empire falls, the alphabet stays. It is already embedded in everything that comes after.

steps.title: The Press
steps.description: Johannes Gutenberg prints the first Bible with movable type in Mainz. For four thousand years, every written word had to be copied by hand. Now a single press can produce hundreds of identical pages. Writing, which began as a tool for accountants, becomes a medium for everyone. Ideas move faster than armies. The Reformation, the Scientific Revolution, the Enlightenment — all ride the press. The way of writing ends here, and something entirely new begins.

steps.title: End of the Holy Roman Empire
steps.description: Napoleon’s armies have already humiliated Austria at Austerlitz. The Confederation of the Rhine pulls sixteen German states out from under imperial authority. On 6 August 1806, Francis II steps to a window in Vienna and reads a declaration: the title of Holy Roman Emperor is laid down, the empire dissolved. A thousand-year political idea ends in a single afternoon. What replaces it is uncertain — a constellation of small kingdoms with no shared roof.

steps.title: Congress of Vienna
steps.description: After Napoleon’s final defeat, Europe’s powers redraw the continent in Vienna. Out of nearly three hundred former territories emerges the German Confederation: 39 states, dominated by Austria and Prussia, loosely yoked together. There is no German nation yet, no common army, no parliament that decides anything. But for the first time since 1806, the word ‘Deutschland’ has a political shape — however thin.

steps.title: Frankfurt Parliament
steps.description: Across Europe revolution flares; in the German lands liberals demand a constitution and a unified state. In Frankfurt’s Paulskirche, 585 elected delegates draft a constitution and offer the imperial crown to the Prussian king. Friedrich Wilhelm IV refuses — a crown ‘from the gutter,’ he calls it. Within a year the parliament is dispersed and the revolution ground down. The unification has to wait, but the idea will not.

steps.title: Proclamation of the German Empire
steps.description: Bismarck has spent a decade engineering this moment: three short wars against Denmark, Austria, and France — each a chess move. After Napoleon III’s defeat at Sedan, the German princes meet in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles and proclaim Wilhelm I as German Emperor. The site is deliberate: in the palace of France’s kings, the new empire is born. The Reich begins as a Prussian-led federation of 25 states, sealed by humiliation.

steps.title: Treaty of Versailles
steps.description: The Great War has consumed an empire. Twenty million dead in Europe, the Hohenzollern crown gone, the Reichstag in republican hands. In the same Hall of Mirrors where the empire was proclaimed, Germany now signs a treaty written by the victors: territorial losses, reparations, the war-guilt clause. The new Weimar Republic begins burdened by a peace it did not negotiate. The treaty closes one chapter and pre-writes the next.

steps.title: Nazi Seizure of Power
steps.description: Inflation, mass unemployment, street violence: Weimar is exhausted. On 30 January 1933 President Hindenburg appoints Hitler as Chancellor — a calculated bet by conservatives that they can contain him. Within months the Reichstag burns, the Enabling Act passes, opposition parties are banned, and Germany has become a one-party dictatorship. The republic dies without a coup, by parliamentary procedure, before an indifferent public.

steps.title: Stunde Null
steps.description: Twelve years of dictatorship and six of war end in May 1945. Forty million dead in Europe alone. Berlin is rubble. The Wehrmacht surrenders unconditionally; the Allies divide the country into four occupation zones. The crimes of the regime — industrial genocide, slave labour, war of annihilation — are uncovered as the camps are liberated. Whatever Germany becomes next must begin from this. The moment is named Stunde Null: zero hour.

steps.title: Two German States
steps.description: Cold War lines harden. In May 1949 the western occupation zones merge into the Federal Republic of Germany, with a new capital at provincial Bonn — chosen so it cannot rival Berlin. Five months later the Soviet zone becomes the German Democratic Republic, with East Berlin as its capital. Two German states face each other across the inner-German border, each claiming to be the legitimate continuation of what came before.

steps.title: Reunification
steps.description: The Berlin Wall has stood for 28 years when, on 9 November 1989, an East German press spokesman misreads new travel rules at a press conference: ‘effective immediately.’ Crowds gather. The guards open the gates. Eleven months later, on 3 October 1990, East Germany dissolves and joins the Federal Republic. After a century shaped by two world wars, partition, and dictatorship, Germany is one country again — and the postwar order it grew up under is ending.

steps.title: The Hijra
steps.description: Mecca will not have him. After twelve years of preaching, persecution, and a slow exodus of his followers, Muhammad himself slips out at night and rides north to Yathrib, a small oasis town whose divided tribes have invited him to mediate their feuds. Within a generation Yathrib will be Medina — the City — and the year of his arrival, 622, will become Year One of a new calendar. Islam is no longer a fringe movement of merchants and outcasts. It is becoming a polity.

steps.title: Conquest of the Near East
steps.description: Three years after Muhammad’s death, his followers ride out of Arabia. The two great empires of the day — Byzantine Rome and Sasanian Persia — have just fought each other to exhaustion. Damascus, a wealthy Roman provincial capital, falls in 635. By 642 Egypt is taken, by 651 Persia is finished. A movement that began with one man in a desert town has, in a single generation, conquered the world’s oldest civilizations. The political map of the Mediterranean is being redrawn faster than anyone can write it down.

steps.title: Battle of Tours
steps.description: A century after the Prophet’s death, Arab armies have crossed the Strait of Gibraltar, taken Spain, and pushed into Frankish territory. Near Poitiers in central France, Charles Martel meets Abd al-Rahman’s cavalry with a wall of infantry. The Arabs break themselves on it. Their commander is killed; the survivors withdraw south. The high-water mark of the western expansion has been reached. Edward Gibbon will later imagine, with some exaggeration, the Quran being taught at Oxford had Martel lost. He didn’t.

steps.title: Baghdad Founded
steps.description: The Abbasid revolution has overthrown the Umayyads and moved the capital eastward. Caliph al-Mansur designs a new city: a perfect circle, walls four kilometres in circumference, four gates pointing to Mecca, Damascus, Khurasan, and Basra. He calls it Madinat as-Salam — the City of Peace — but everyone calls it Baghdad. Within a century it will be the largest city on Earth, a magnet for Greek philosophy, Indian mathematics, Persian poetry. The Islamic Golden Age has its capital.

steps.title: Caliphate of Córdoba
steps.description: When the Abbasids took Baghdad, one Umayyad prince escaped — west, to Spain. Two centuries later his descendant Abd al-Rahman III declares himself caliph in Córdoba, breaking the formal unity of the Muslim world. By the year 1000 Córdoba is the largest city in Western Europe: half a million people, hundreds of mosques, a library said to hold 400,000 volumes when Christian Europe’s libraries hold a few hundred. Aristotle returns to the Latin West through here. So do paper, algebra, and the lute.

steps.title: Battle of Manzikert
steps.description: The Seljuk Turks — recent converts from the steppe — have built an empire stretching from Central Asia to the Levant. At Manzikert in eastern Anatolia, they meet a Byzantine army personally led by Emperor Romanos IV. The Byzantines are crushed; Romanos is captured. The defeat opens Anatolia, the empire’s heartland, to Turkish settlement. Within a generation what had been Greek-speaking, Christian Asia Minor for a thousand years is becoming something else. The empire never recovers, and a panicked emperor will write to the Pope for help.

steps.title: Crusaders Take Jerusalem
steps.description: Pope Urban II’s call at Clermont in 1095 has produced something nobody quite anticipated: an army of tens of thousands marching across Europe, through Constantinople, across Anatolia, down the Levantine coast. After three years on the road, after famine and battle and intra-Christian betrayal, they arrive at Jerusalem. The siege lasts five weeks. When the walls fall on 15 July, the slaughter is general. A Frankish kingdom is established along the Levantine coast. The encounter the West has been having with Islam from a distance has just become very immediate.

steps.title: Saladin Retakes Jerusalem
steps.description: For 88 years the Crusader states cling to the Levantine coast, while Muslim power around them slowly consolidates. Salah ad-Din — Saladin — Kurdish general, Sultan of Egypt and Syria, finally unites the fragmented response. At the Horns of Hattin in July 1187 he annihilates the Crusader field army; three months later Jerusalem opens its gates. The contrast with 1099 is deliberate: Saladin lets the Christian inhabitants ransom themselves, frees the poor without payment. Europe will mythologize him afterward as the model of a chivalrous enemy.

steps.title: Fall of Constantinople
steps.description: Mehmed II is 21 years old. The walls of Constantinople have stood since the time of Theodosius — over a thousand years. He brings to them an artillery train no city has ever faced: cannons cast specifically to break this stone. After 53 days the breach holds; Ottoman troops pour in. Constantine XI dies fighting in the streets. The Hagia Sophia becomes a mosque. Mehmed begins styling himself Kaisar-i Rum — Caesar of Rome. Byzantium, the eastern half of the Roman Empire that lasted a millennium beyond the western, has finally ended. The successor is the Ottoman Empire.

steps.title: Battle of Vienna
steps.description: The Ottomans have been pressing into Central Europe for two centuries. In summer 1683 an army of 150,000 under Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa lays siege to Vienna — for the second time, after 1529. The city holds for two months. On 12 September a Polish-led relief force charges down from the Kahlenberg hills and breaks the siege. The Ottoman tents are looted; among the trophies, supposedly, sacks of green beans the Viennese will turn into a coffee culture. The empire’s high tide is over. From now on the line moves the other way.
